They wanted southern governments to meet national conditions before receiving full representation. Congress therefore favored a longer process, stronger federal supervision, and a definition of citizenship that states could not easily remove. It also wanted political participation for formerly enslaved men.
Presidents claimed that restoring states was an executive responsibility, while legislators claimed authority because each chamber decided whether to seat representatives.
analysis_reasoning: In practice, neither approach fully secured a stable settlement. Leniency allowed hostile local officials to regain influence, while stricter congressional control depended on continuing northern commitment.
The comparison is therefore not simply kind against cruel.

prompt: Develop an argument comparing systems of racial control in the New South and the trans-Mississippi West from 1877 to 1898.
student_response:
Racial control is when one group has control over another race and this was around in many parts of America. The New South was called new because the Civil War was over and the West was new because people kept finding land there. Both areas had many populations, although not always the same populations, and the government had to decide what to do.

In the South, people were rebuilding cities and farms that had been ruined. Some former workers left farms, some stayed, and other people moved into towns. There were elections but also arguments over who was allowed to be involved. The North sent southern soldiers to supervise for a while, and later those soldiers moved west. This made the South more modern, but also more traditional at the same time. Rules were different in every state and many people did not obey them. Violence was also a problem when groups disagreed.

The trans-Missisippi West covered a huge area. There were farmers, miners, cattle people, immigrants, and native tribes. They fought for water and land because the climate was dry. The government put groups in seperate areas so there would be less fighting, but there was still fighting. Some people were made citizens and other people were considered foreigners even when they were born there. The Spanish had built missions after the Civil War and these controlled western cities for the federal government.

There are many comparisons that could be made. The South had a warmer climate than most of the West, the West had more mountains, and both had railroads. Race effected where people could live and work, although class and gender mattered to. Sometimes control came from government and sometimes it did not. Eventually the areas became more equal, but this did not happen all at once. It is hard to say whether the systems were the same or different because each person had a different experiance and there were good and bad laws in both places.

feedback.thesis: The response does not make a defensible comparison of the two systems and explicitly declines to determine how they were similar or different.
feedback.contextualization: The opening supplies only generic descriptions of the regions rather than relevant broader historical context for racial control after 1877.
feedback.evidence: The essay offers broad references to elections, violence, land, and separated groups, but no sufficiently specific historical examples of racial-control systems in either region.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response earns the reasoning point because it organizes discussion around comparison, noting possible similarities and differences in government and nongovernment control, even though the comparison remains vague.

thesis: It is hard to say whether the systems were the same or different because each person had a different experiance and there were good and bad laws in both places.
contextualization: The New South was called new because the Civil War was over and the West was new because people kept finding land there.
evidence: There were elections but also arguments over who was allowed to be involved.
The government put groups in seperate areas so there would be less fighting, but there was still fighting.
analysis_reasoning: Sometimes control came from government and sometimes it did not.

feedback.thesis: The response does not make a clear, defensible claim comparing the two systems of racial control and ultimately refuses a comparison.
feedback.contextualization: The opening gives only vague descriptions of the postwar South and western land rather than meaningful broader context for racial control.
feedback.evidence: The essay provides no specific historical examples of racial-control systems in either region; its references to rules, separate areas, and violence remain unidentified.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: Although it lists surface similarities and differences, the response does not use comparison to explain how systems of racial control operated in the two regions.

thesis: It is hard to say whether the systems were the same or different because each person had a different experiance and there were good and bad laws in both places.
contextualization: The New South was called new because the Civil War was over and the West was new because people kept finding land there.
evidence: Rules were different in every state and many people did not obey them.
The government put groups in seperate areas so there would be less fighting, but there was still fighting.
analysis_reasoning: The South had a warmer climate than most of the West, the West had more mountains, and both had railroads.

prompt: Develop an argument explaining the causes of Progressive reform from 1890 to 1920.
student_response:
By the late nineteenth century the United States had changed from a mostly rural society into one with giant corporations, crowded industrial cities, and millions of wage workers. Immigration supplied labor, railroads tied together a national market, and political machines often provided services while trading favors for votes. These developments left many Americans worried that older government was not able to deal with modern conditions.

There were many reformers with many complaints. Some worried about poverty and tenements, others about political corruption, alcohol, unsafe food, or the power of trusts. Middle-class professionals believed experts could study these problems. Workers wanted safer factories and shorter hours. Women who had experience in clubs and charitable work became active in public campaigns even though they could not yet vote nationally. Farmers also disliked railroad and banking power.

The Triangle Shirtwaist Factory fire in 1911 killed workers who could not escape the building. Investigators and reformers discussed factory inspections and safety rules afterward. It was one of the famous events of the era. There were also elections, magazines, city investigations, and speeches about reform. Different states acted at different speeds and reform did not mean the same thing to everyone. Some reformers cared about democracy while others tried to control immigrants and the poor. Progressive reform came out of this whole collection of issues, leaders, and events, which makes the subject complicated and there is not really one explanation that fits it all.

feedback.thesis: The response argues that modern industrial and urban conditions produced many complaints that older government could not address, establishing a broad causal line.
feedback.contextualization: The opening accurately frames reform amid corporations, industrial cities, wage labor, immigration, railroads, and political machines.
feedback.evidence: The Triangle fire and ensuing safety discussion, political-machine practices, women's club activity, trusts, tenements, and worker demands are used as examples of pressures for reform.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response uses causation by connecting varied industrial-era problems and constituencies to reform, but it does not develop a sufficiently sophisticated or evidence-rich explanation for the second point.

thesis: These developments left many Americans worried that older government was not able to deal with modern conditions.
Progressive reform came out of this whole collection of issues, leaders, and events
contextualization: By the late nineteenth century the United States had changed from a mostly rural society into one with giant corporations, crowded industrial cities, and millions of wage workers.
evidence: The Triangle Shirtwaist Factory fire in 1911 killed workers who could not escape the building.
Investigators and reformers discussed factory inspections and safety rules afterward.
Women who had experience in clubs and charitable work became active in public campaigns even though they could not yet vote nationally.
analysis_reasoning: Some worried about poverty and tenements, others about political corruption, alcohol, unsafe food, or the power of trusts.

feedback.thesis: The response lists many complaints but does not establish a specific causal claim or line of reasoning about which factors produced Progressive reform.
feedback.contextualization: The opening accurately frames reform through industrialization, urbanization, immigration, national markets, and political machines.
feedback.evidence: The Triangle fire is one specific example, but the rubric requires at least two specific examples for the evidence point.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response uses causal reasoning by linking modern industrial and urban problems, reform constituencies, and the Triangle fire to demands for reform.

thesis: Progressive reform came out of this whole collection of issues, leaders, and events, which makes the subject complicated and there is not really one explanation that fits it all.
contextualization: By the late nineteenth century the United States had changed from a mostly rural society into one with giant corporations, crowded industrial cities, and millions of wage workers.
evidence: The Triangle Shirtwaist Factory fire in 1911 killed workers who could not escape the building.
analysis_reasoning: Investigators and reformers discussed factory inspections and safety rules afterward.

prompt: Develop an argument evaluating continuity and change in federal restrictions on civil liberties during wartime from 1917 to 1945.
student_response:
Before the twentieth century, the government had limited speech in earlier emergencies, such as the Alien and Sedition laws and Lincoln's suspension of habeas corpus during the Civil War. World war again raised the question of whether individual rights survived a national crisis. From 1917 to 1945 federal restrictions remained continuous because officials punished people considered dangerous during both wars, but the main focus changed from political dissent in World War I toward groups treated as enemy races in World War II.

In World War I, Congress enacted the Espionage Act of 1917. It could be used against people who interfered with the draft or opposed the war. Newspapers and speakers had to be careful and the Post Office could keep certain publications out of the mail. The Supreme Court passed a rule about dangerous speech, it meant normal speech could become illegal in wartime. A person might be punished even without directly helping an enemy. People disagreed about whether this protected soldiers or just protected leaders from criticism, and the answer was not clear during the emergency.

During the second war the government was less focused on ordinary antiwar speeches but more focused on people's family backgrounds. Officials removed families from communities without individually proving that each person had done something wrong. Property and freedom could be lost. The policy was described as military safety. There were also investigations, loyalty worries, and many rumors, citizens did not always know which rumors were accurate.

Civil liberties include speech, privacy, fair trials, and equal treatment. Not every liberty was restricted in the same way. Elections still happened, newspapers still printed stories, and citizens continued to criticize officials, so the whole country was not exactly a dictatorship. At the same time, being at war made restrictions easier for the public to accept.

The years from the first war to the second war contained both kinds of federal action. Some restrictions were about words and opinions, some were about who officials thought might be dangerous. Fear was present and the government had more power. The details changed, but civil liberties were under pressure whenever leaders said national safety was involved.

feedback.thesis: The thesis identifies continuity in wartime punishment and a change from suppressing dissent to targeting people by perceived enemy ancestry.
feedback.contextualization: The essay situates the period within earlier emergency restrictions by referencing the Alien and Sedition laws and Civil War suspension of habeas corpus.
feedback.evidence: The Espionage Act, draft opposition, postal restrictions, wartime speech rules, and removal of families without individual proof support the continuity-and-change argument.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response sustains continuity and change while adding nuance about different liberties, continued elections and criticism, and the distinction between repression and total dictatorship.

thesis: From 1917 to 1945 federal restrictions remained continuous because officials punished people considered dangerous during both wars, but the main focus changed from political dissent in World War I toward groups treated as enemy races in World War II.
contextualization: Before the twentieth century, the government had limited speech in earlier emergencies, such as the Alien and Sedition laws and Lincoln's suspension of habeas corpus during the Civil War.
evidence: In World War I, Congress enacted the Espionage Act of 1917.
the Post Office could keep certain publications out of the mail.
Officials removed families from communities without individually proving that each person had done something wrong.
analysis_reasoning: Elections still happened, newspapers still printed stories, and citizens continued to criticize officials, so the whole country was not exactly a dictatorship.

feedback.thesis: The thesis clearly identifies continuity in wartime punishment and change from political dissent to racialized suspicion.
feedback.contextualization: Alien and Sedition laws and Lincoln's suspension of habeas corpus provide relevant pre-1917 context.
feedback.evidence: The Espionage Act, draft opposition, postal restrictions, dangerous-speech doctrine, and family removals support the stated continuity and change.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The essay structures its argument through continuity and change, but its limited identification and development of World War II evidence does not demonstrate sufficiently sophisticated understanding for the second point.

thesis: From 1917 to 1945 federal restrictions remained continuous because officials punished people considered dangerous during both wars, but the main focus changed from political dissent in World War I toward groups treated as enemy races in World War II.
contextualization: Before the twentieth century, the government had limited speech in earlier emergencies, such as the Alien and Sedition laws and Lincoln's suspension of habeas corpus during the Civil War.
evidence: In World War I, Congress enacted the Espionage Act of 1917.
Officials removed families from communities without individually proving that each person had done something wrong.
analysis_reasoning: The details changed, but civil liberties were under pressure whenever leaders said national safety was involved.

prompt: Develop an argument evaluating continuity and change in Indigenous diplomatic strategies toward European colonies from 1607 to 1754.
student_response:
European colonies entered a continent already occupied by numerous Indigenous nations with their own trade routes, rivalries, and political traditions. Because English, French, Dutch, and Spanish settlements competed for land and commerce, Native leaders could sometimes use imperial rivalry to defend autonomy. From 1607 to 1754 Indigenous diplomacy continuously sought advantageous alliances and a balance among outsiders, but it changed as settler population and land pressure increased, making broader Native coordination and the threat of military resistance more urgent than the smaller exchanges of the early colonial period.

Diplomacy could take many forms. Leaders met, exchanged gifts, delivered speeches, selected interpreters, and carried messages back to their communities. A meeting was not only two individuals talking. Each speaker represented relatives and other leaders who might reject an agreement. Words also had to be translated, and a translated word might not carry exactly the same meaning. Agreements could concern trade, travel, land, prisoners, or military help. Everybody wanted the same European products, so every Native community always made the same diplomatic choice.

Ceremony mattered. Participants might remember an older promise before talking about a new dispute. Gifts showed respect but could also create an obligation. Colonial officials sometimes wanted a written paper with a fixed boundary. Indigenous speakers could emphasize an ongoing relationship that needed to be renewed. This difference created confusion. Sometimes the confusion was probably accidental and sometimes a negotiator used it for an advantage.

The Covenant Chain was one diplomatic arrangement involving the Haudenosaunee and English colonies. It was discussed in councils and represented as a relationship that required attention. Different participants could understand the arrangement differently. It is one example of Native people appearing as diplomats rather than simply reacting to whatever colonists decided.

Leaders had other choices as well. They might delay an answer, send a new delegation, move a community, allow some trade, reject a missionary, or warn that warfare could follow. Neutrality could be a strategy. So could alliance. So could refusing to commit until it became clearer which outsider offered better terms. A leader who made a successful agreement could gain influence, while a failed agreement could lead to criticism at home.

Diplomacy did not mean equality. Colonists wanted land and traders wanted profit. Native communities wanted security but did not all define security in the same way. Some lived near colonies and others farther away. Some had enemies who seemed like a greater immediate danger than Europeans. All tribes joined together whenever Europeans threatened one of them, although at other times they disagreed.

Indigenous diplomacy from 1607 to 1754 therefore included meetings, ceremonies, alliances, threats, and efforts at neutrality. The methods could overlap, and an agreement might work for a time without solving every conflict. There was continuity and change during these years, but the many communities and local situations make the details difficult to put into one pattern.

feedback.thesis: The thesis clearly identifies continuity in balancing outsiders and change toward broader coordination and threatened resistance as land pressure grew.
feedback.contextualization: The response establishes Indigenous political traditions and trade networks and explains how competing European empires created diplomatic opportunities.
feedback.evidence: Gift exchange, interpreters, councils, boundaries, the Covenant Chain, neutrality, alliances, delegations, trade, and threats provide multiple concrete examples of diplomatic strategy.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: Continuity and change structure the response, but broad unsupported claims and limited evidence for the asserted shift toward broader coordination prevent complex understanding.

thesis: From 1607 to 1754 Indigenous diplomacy continuously sought advantageous alliances and a balance among outsiders, but it changed as settler population and land pressure increased, making broader Native coordination and the threat of military resistance more urgent than the smaller exchanges of the early colonial period.
contextualization: European colonies entered a continent already occupied by numerous Indigenous nations with their own trade routes, rivalries, and political traditions.
evidence: Leaders met, exchanged gifts, delivered speeches, selected interpreters, and carried messages back to their communities.
The Covenant Chain was one diplomatic arrangement involving the Haudenosaunee and English colonies.
Neutrality could be a strategy. So could alliance.
analysis_reasoning: Indigenous diplomacy from 1607 to 1754 therefore included meetings, ceremonies, alliances, threats, and efforts at neutrality.

feedback.thesis: The thesis defensibly identifies continuity in alliance balancing and change toward broader coordination and threatened resistance under greater land pressure.
feedback.contextualization: The opening situates diplomacy among preexisting Native nations and competing English, French, Dutch, and Spanish settlements.
feedback.evidence: The Covenant Chain is the only specific historical example; generic ceremonies, gifts, messages, neutrality, alliances, and threats do not meet the two-example threshold.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response uses continuity-and-change language and compares diplomatic methods, but the body does not concretely trace the claimed change over time.

thesis: From 1607 to 1754 Indigenous diplomacy continuously sought advantageous alliances and a balance among outsiders, but it changed as settler population and land pressure increased, making broader Native coordination and the threat of military resistance more urgent than the smaller exchanges of the early colonial period.
contextualization: European colonies entered a continent already occupied by numerous Indigenous nations with their own trade routes, rivalries, and political traditions.
evidence: The Covenant Chain was one diplomatic arrangement involving the Haudenosaunee and English colonies.
analysis_reasoning: Indigenous diplomacy from 1607 to 1754 therefore included meetings, ceremonies, alliances, threats, and efforts at neutrality.

prompt: Develop an argument explaining the most important causes of the emergence of the first party system from 1787 to 1800.
student_response: The first political parties appeared soon after the Constitution, but the reasons are hard to rank because almost every dispute became political. Before 1787 the Articles of Confederation had left Congress unable to tax or regulate commerce. Shays' Rebellion frightened property owners, while other Americans feared that a new central government could become another British monarchy. Ratification therefore already left Federalists and Anti-Federalists suspicious of each other. Once the new government started operating, those old suspicions did not disappear. Hamilton wrote a financial program involving assumption of state debts, funding the national debt, and a national bank. Jefferson and Madison opposed much of it. The bank was especially disputed because Hamilton used a strict interpretation of the Constitution while Jefferson defended implied powers, which was really the reverse of their usual positions. Cabinet arguments then spread into newspapers such as the Gazette of the United States and the National Gazette. Foreign events made the arguments louder. The French Revolution divided Americans who admired republican France from those alarmed by its violence. Washington's Neutrality Proclamation and Jay's Treaty also caused controversy. Democratic-Republican societies criticized the administration, and Federalists thought the societies encouraged disorder like the Whiskey Rebellion. Later the XYZ Affair helped produce the Alien and Sedition Acts, and the Virginia and Kentucky Resolutions answered them. There were also personality problems. Hamilton distrusted Jefferson, Jefferson distrusted Hamilton, and Adams did not always agree with other Federalists. Elections rewarded people for organizing supporters, printing attacks, and identifying candidates with a side. By 1796 voters were choosing between groups even though the founders often said factions were dangerous. Party labels also appeared in congressional voting and local meetings. Candidates depended on letters, printers, tavern discussions, and allies in state legislatures because presidential electors were chosen differently from state to state. Not yet a modern campaign system. The election of 1800 then became especially bitter, with accusations going in every direction. All of these developments mattered. Economic plans, constitutional disagreements, newspapers, France, Britain, and ambitious leaders each played a role, so it is not really possible to identify a most important cause. The parties emerged because disagreements existed and people eventually took sides. In the end the Federalists generally favored order and the Republicans generally favored liberty, although both sides claimed both ideas. A political system that had been designed without parties somehow had parties by 1800.

feedback.thesis: The response names many causes but explicitly declines to identify or defend a most important cause, ending with a generic claim that disagreements led people to take sides.
feedback.contextualization: The discussion of the Articles of Confederation, Shays' Rebellion, and ratification disputes accurately frames the political tensions preceding the first party system.
feedback.evidence: Specific examples including Hamilton's financial program, rival newspapers, the French Revolution, Jay's Treaty, and the Alien and Sedition Acts are connected to the growth of partisan conflict.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The essay uses causal reasoning by grouping economic, constitutional, foreign-policy, and personal disputes, but it mostly catalogs them and does not develop a complex ranking or interaction among causes.

thesis: All of these developments mattered. Economic plans, constitutional disagreements, newspapers, France, Britain, and ambitious leaders each played a role, so it is not really possible to identify a most important cause.
contextualization: Before 1787 the Articles of Confederation had left Congress unable to tax or regulate commerce.
evidence: Hamilton wrote a financial program involving assumption of state debts, funding the national debt, and a national bank.
analysis_reasoning: Foreign events made the arguments louder.

feedback.thesis: The conclusion identifies several contributing categories but explicitly declines to establish which cause was most important, so it does not supply the requested defensible priority or a clear line of reasoning.
feedback.contextualization: The discussion of the Articles of Confederation, Shays' Rebellion, and ratification disputes accurately frames the political suspicions that preceded the first party system.
feedback.evidence: The response uses numerous specific examples—including Hamilton's financial program, rival newspapers, the French Revolution, Jay's Treaty, and the Alien and Sedition Acts—to explain why alignments hardened.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: Causal reasoning links economic, constitutional, foreign-policy, media, and electoral developments to party formation; more than four pieces of evidence are used effectively even though the essay does not rank them.

thesis: All of these developments mattered. Economic plans, constitutional disagreements, newspapers, France, Britain, and ambitious leaders each played a role, so it is not really possible to identify a most important cause.
contextualization: Before 1787 the Articles of Confederation had left Congress unable to tax or regulate commerce. Shays' Rebellion frightened property owners, while other Americans feared that a new central government could become another British monarchy.
evidence: Hamilton wrote a financial program involving assumption of state debts, funding the national debt, and a national bank.
The French Revolution divided Americans who admired republican France from those alarmed by its violence. Washington's Neutrality Proclamation and Jay's Treaty also caused controversy.
analysis_reasoning: Cabinet arguments then spread into newspapers such as the Gazette of the United States and the National Gazette.
Elections rewarded people for organizing supporters, printing attacks, and identifying candidates with a side.

prompt: Develop an argument evaluating continuity and change in federal economic policy from 1800 to 1848.
student_response: From 1800 to 1848, federal economic policy changed from a stated preference for limited national action toward broader federal promotion of growth, but it continuously provoked disagreement over whether economic development belonged to the nation or to the states. The greatest change was the increasing expectation that public authority should help connect regions and encourage commerce, while the strongest continuity was suspicion that national economic power would favor one section or class. Economic policy affected banking, transportation, trade, public land, and government revenue. National leaders had to decide whether one policy could fit agricultural districts, commercial ports, and newer settlements. Some citizens wanted stable credit and coordinated development. Others believed that distant officials would make rules benefiting financiers and manufacturers. These positions did not remain attached permanently to one region or one political label. A policy could seem useful when it aided local producers and unconstitutional when its costs appeared elsewhere. Transportation illustrates the dispute in a general way. Better routes lowered shipping time and linked farmers to wider markets, so demands for public support increased as settlement moved farther inland. Yet arguments continued about who should pay, whether projects were local or national, and which communities would receive the gains. Similar disagreements surrounded banking. A broad financial institution could make payments more uniform and assist government finance, but opponents saw concentrated privilege and worried that credit decisions escaped popular control. Tariff policy raised another sectional argument because protection could assist domestic production while making imported goods more expensive and inviting retaliation against exports. These recurring controversies show why economic policy expanded unevenly. The federal government was not simply growing in a straight line, and it was not simply retreating. National officials sometimes accepted developmental aims but rejected a particular method. At other times, state governments or private investors performed work that supporters originally imagined as national. The result by 1848 was a much more connected national economy alongside an unresolved constitutional debate. Federal policy had helped create conditions for growth in some ways, but Americans continued to resist policies they interpreted as special privilege. Therefore both change and continuity characterized the era: expectations for economic development grew, while the political conflict over the legitimate reach of national economic authority remained remarkably durable.

feedback.thesis: The thesis clearly evaluates change toward broader federal promotion of growth and continuity in conflict over legitimate national economic power.
feedback.contextualization: The response begins with its period argument and general policy dilemmas rather than describing a broader development preceding or framing the period.
feedback.evidence: Transportation, banking, and tariffs are discussed only as general policy categories; the essay provides no specific historical program, law, institution, leader, or event to substantiate the argument.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response offers sophisticated continuity-and-change reasoning by rejecting linear growth, distinguishing federal aims from methods, and explaining the roles of states and private investors.

thesis: From 1800 to 1848, federal economic policy changed from a stated preference for limited national action toward broader federal promotion of growth, but it continuously provoked disagreement over whether economic development belonged to the nation or to the states.
contextualization: Economic policy affected banking, transportation, trade, public land, and government revenue.
evidence: Transportation illustrates the dispute in a general way.
analysis_reasoning: The federal government was not simply growing in a straight line, and it was not simply retreating. National officials sometimes accepted developmental aims but rejected a particular method. At other times, state governments or private investors performed work that supporters originally imagined as national.

feedback.thesis: The opening clearly argues both change toward broader federal promotion and continuity in conflict over national versus state authority.
feedback.contextualization: The response discusses economic sectors and viewpoints within the period but does not describe a broader development outside the prompt's immediate chronology that frames the argument.
feedback.evidence: Banking, transportation, tariffs, and public land remain unnamed policy categories; the essay supplies no two specific historical examples required for an evidence point.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: Continuity-and-change reasoning consistently structures the response, especially its explanation that policy expanded unevenly and constitutional conflict persisted, but the absence of concrete evidence prevents the higher point.

thesis: From 1800 to 1848, federal economic policy changed from a stated preference for limited national action toward broader federal promotion of growth, but it continuously provoked disagreement over whether economic development belonged to the nation or to the states.
contextualization: Economic policy affected banking, transportation, trade, public land, and government revenue.
evidence: A broad financial institution could make payments more uniform and assist government finance, but opponents saw concentrated privilege and worried that credit decisions escaped popular control.
Tariff policy raised another sectional argument because protection could assist domestic production while making imported goods more expensive and inviting retaliation against exports.
analysis_reasoning: The federal government was not simply growing in a straight line, and it was not simply retreating. National officials sometimes accepted developmental aims but rejected a particular method.

prompt: Develop an argument explaining the most important causes of the market revolution from 1800 to 1848.
student_response: Before 1800, most American families lived near farms and exchanged many goods in local markets. Roads were poor, travel was slow, and the different regions were not always connected. Population growth and settlement after independence created pressure for a larger economy. The market revolution came from many developments, and every development was important in its own way, so picking one main cause does not really explain it. Transportation changed during this period. The Erie Canal connected western farms to New York City, while the National Road and steamboats also made movement easier. These projects are examples of internal improvments. Railroads began to appear and eventually became even more important. New machines were another part of the story. Eli Whitney's cotton gin cleaned cotton faster. Textile mills gathered workers and machines in one building, including the women who worked at Lowell. There was also more immigration, which gave growing cities additional labor. Farmers could send crops farther away instead of only using them at home. Factories sold manufactured products, and banks provided money. Telegraph lines later allowed information to move quickly. Each invention or group added something to a changing market. The North made more manufactured goods, the South grew cotton, and western farmers produced food. People moved between these areas and bought things made by strangers. Workers increasingly earned wages instead of producing everything for their own family. In conclusion the market revolution had transportation, inventions, workers, farms, factories, and trade. All of them occured between 1800 and 1848 and together the economy became a market revolution.

feedback.thesis: The essay explicitly avoids identifying a most important cause and substitutes a list of developments for a defensible prioritization.
feedback.contextualization: The account of local exchange, poor roads, slow travel, population growth, and post-independence settlement accurately frames the market revolution.
feedback.evidence: The Erie Canal, National Road, steamboats, cotton gin, Lowell mills, immigration, and telegraph are used to explain expanding production and exchange.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response uses causal reasoning by organizing transportation, technology, labor, and regional specialization as contributors, but it does not develop hierarchy, interaction, or qualification sufficiently for complexity.

thesis: The market revolution came from many developments, and every development was important in its own way, so picking one main cause does not really explain it.
contextualization: Before 1800, most American families lived near farms and exchanged many goods in local markets. Roads were poor, travel was slow, and the different regions were not always connected.
evidence: The Erie Canal connected western farms to New York City, while the National Road and steamboats also made movement easier.
analysis_reasoning: The North made more manufactured goods, the South grew cotton, and western farmers produced food. People moved between these areas and bought things made by strangers.

feedback.thesis: The response says every development mattered and declines to identify a leading cause, so it does not meet the prompt's request to explain the most important causes through a defensible priority.
feedback.contextualization: The description of local exchange, poor roads, slow travel, regional disconnection, population growth, and post-independence settlement effectively frames the market revolution.
feedback.evidence: The Erie Canal, National Road, steamboats, railroads, cotton gin, Lowell mills, immigration, banks, and telegraph are specific examples connected to expanding markets and production.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: Causal reasoning links transportation, technology, labor, finance, and regional specialization to market growth, and the essay effectively uses more than four pieces of evidence despite not ranking causes.

thesis: The market revolution came from many developments, and every development was important in its own way, so picking one main cause does not really explain it.
contextualization: Before 1800, most American families lived near farms and exchanged many goods in local markets. Roads were poor, travel was slow, and the different regions were not always connected.
evidence: The Erie Canal connected western farms to New York City, while the National Road and steamboats also made movement easier.
Eli Whitney's cotton gin cleaned cotton faster. Textile mills gathered workers and machines in one building, including the women who worked at Lowell.
analysis_reasoning: Farmers could send crops farther away instead of only using them at home.
The North made more manufactured goods, the South grew cotton, and western farmers produced food. People moved between these areas and bought things made by strangers.

prompt: Develop an argument explaining the causes of the decline of Reconstruction governments from 1868 to 1877.
student_response: Reconstruction governments declined mainly because white southern resistance combined with declining northern willingness to keep spending political and military energy on racial equality, while economic worries drew public attention elsewhere. These forces made the governments easier to portray as illegitimate and harder to defend. Reconstruction governments were controversial because the country did not agree about what equality required. Some citizens believed any federal involvement in local voting was dictatorship. Others believed national protection was necessary wherever a majority threatened a minority. This disagreement continued for years and made every election feel temporary. Southern opponents used speeches, social pressure, and claims about local self-government to make cooperation look like betrayal. Northern voters gradually became less interested. They wanted political stability and worried about taxes, jobs, and charges that public officials were dishonest. Stories of corruption were treated as proof that the entire effort had failed, even though corruption can exist under any party. The debate was also influenced by later examples of civil rights conflict. In Brown v. Board of Education, the Supreme Court declared segregated schools unconstitutional, but states resisted and forced the national government to intervene. The Montgomery Bus Boycott showed how organized Black communities could challenge local rules when ordinary politics did not respond. Television coverage of violence in Birmingham finally convinced many northern viewers that federal civil rights laws were needed. These events reveal why Reconstruction governments lost support: Americans do not maintain attention to civil rights unless dramatic images force them to notice. Economic policy also changed priorities. During the New Deal the federal government created relief programs and regulations because unemployment had become more important than older sectional questions. Politicians learned that promises about wages and economic security attract more votes than arguments about constitutional rights. That same public tendency hurt Reconstruction governments once people became tired of conflict. There were also accusations that inexperienced voters and officials could not govern responsibly. Such accusations gave opponents a respectable sounding reason for recovering power without openly saying they opposed equality. Because government authority depends on people accepting election results, constant claims that an administration is corrupt can weaken it even when some of its policies are useful. Eventually the national government preferred reconciliation between white northerners and southerners. The governments then had fewer allies and could not survive resistance on their own. In this sense their decline was not caused by one bad law or leader. It came from organized hostility in the South and political exhaustion in the North, with economic distraction accelerating both.

feedback.thesis: The thesis clearly argues that organized white southern resistance and declining northern commitment were the main causes, with economic concerns accelerating decline.
feedback.contextualization: The response does not establish broader historical circumstances preceding or framing Reconstruction government decline; it moves from the thesis into general disagreement.
feedback.evidence: The essay offers no specific in-period law, election, organization, economic crisis, leader, or event; its named examples concern later civil-rights and economic episodes rather than 1868–1877.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The causal reasoning is sophisticated: it explains interacting southern hostility, northern exhaustion, legitimacy claims, economic distraction, and the political effect of corruption accusations.

thesis: Reconstruction governments declined mainly because white southern resistance combined with declining northern willingness to keep spending political and military energy on racial equality, while economic worries drew public attention elsewhere.
contextualization: Reconstruction governments were controversial because the country did not agree about what equality required.
evidence: The debate was also influenced by later examples of civil rights conflict.
analysis_reasoning: In this sense their decline was not caused by one bad law or leader. It came from organized hostility in the South and political exhaustion in the North, with economic distraction accelerating both.

feedback.thesis: The opening provides a defensible causal hierarchy combining organized white southern resistance, declining northern commitment, and economic distraction.
feedback.contextualization: The general disagreement over equality is part of the Reconstruction controversy itself, while the later civil-rights and New Deal material does not provide appropriate broader context for 1868–1877.
feedback.evidence: The essay names no specific Reconstruction-era event, law, organization, election, or economic crisis; its specific examples come from much later periods and cannot substantiate the prompt's chronology.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response structures an argument around interacting political, social, and economic causes, but the absence of period-specific evidence and extended later analogies prevent complex historical analysis.

thesis: Reconstruction governments declined mainly because white southern resistance combined with declining northern willingness to keep spending political and military energy on racial equality, while economic worries drew public attention elsewhere.
contextualization: Reconstruction governments were controversial because the country did not agree about what equality required.
evidence: In Brown v. Board of Education, the Supreme Court declared segregated schools unconstitutional, but states resisted and forced the national government to intervene.
During the New Deal the federal government created relief programs and regulations because unemployment had become more important than older sectional questions.
analysis_reasoning: It came from organized hostility in the South and political exhaustion in the North, with economic distraction accelerating both.

prompt: Develop an argument explaining the causes of the modern environmental movement from 1945 to 1980.
student_response:
The modern environmental movement grew because pollution became more visible, ordinary people became more aware that nature could be damaged, and the government was not doing enough to protect public health. These causes made environmentalism change from just enjoying nature into a political movement that demanded rules for companies and cleaner communities.

After the big war the country was making more products and people were buying cars and moving into growing places, this was good for the economy but it also made the air and water worse, and a lot of people started noticing it around the 1950s or maybe the 1970s. Factories could throw things away and nobody really knew what chemicals did. Some rivers were dirty and cities had smog. People who lived nearby did not like the smell and worried about children. This led them to protest because nature belongs to everyone and not only businesses.

Another cause was new information. Writers, scientists, and television showed that human actions could hurt animals and eventually people too, and once the public saw pictures it was hard to ignore. Young people were already questioning authority and they carried that attitude into environmental issues, there were marches and groups, although many of them did not agree about what problem should be fixed first. Some cared about wilderness and some cared about dangerous products in neighborhoods.

The government then became involved because voters wanted action. New offices and laws were made sometime in this period to watch pollution, however companies complained that rules cost jobs. This debate actually made the movement more political since environmentalists had to convince officials instead of only cleaning parks. There were also oil problems and people realized resources could run out, so conservation became popular again.

Overall the movement was caused by growing pollution, greater public knowledge, and distrust of businesses and government, these factors worked together as America became more modern. It was not caused by one disaster exactly, it was a general realization over several decades that economic growth could have harmful effects.

feedback.thesis: Clearly argues that visible pollution, public awareness, and inadequate government protection caused environmentalism to become political.
feedback.contextualization: Situates the movement in postwar production, automobile use, urban growth, and their environmental consequences.
feedback.evidence: Uses relevant examples such as factory waste, dirty rivers, urban smog, public demonstrations, pollution laws, and oil problems to support the argument.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: Uses causation throughout, explaining how pollution, information, activism, regulation, and resource concerns contributed to growth, but does not reach complex analysis.

thesis: The modern environmental movement grew because pollution became more visible, ordinary people became more aware that nature could be damaged, and the government was not doing enough to protect public health.
contextualization: After the big war the country was making more products and people were buying cars and moving into growing places, this was good for the economy but it also made the air and water worse
evidence: Some rivers were dirty and cities had smog.
There were also oil problems and people realized resources could run out, so conservation became popular again.
analysis_reasoning: Overall the movement was caused by growing pollution, greater public knowledge, and distrust of businesses and government, these factors worked together as America became more modern.

feedback.thesis: The introduction gives a defensible causal claim linking visible pollution, public awareness, and insufficient government protection to movement growth.
feedback.contextualization: The essay frames environmentalism within postwar production, automobile use, and urban growth that worsened air and water conditions.
feedback.evidence: It mentions dirty rivers, smog, media, laws, and oil problems only generally, without at least two sufficiently specific historical examples.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The paragraphs consistently use causation to connect pollution, information, and voter pressure to political environmentalism.

thesis: The modern environmental movement grew because pollution became more visible, ordinary people became more aware that nature could be damaged, and the government was not doing enough to protect public health.
contextualization: After the big war the country was making more products and people were buying cars and moving into growing places, this was good for the economy but it also made the air and water worse
evidence: Some rivers were dirty and cities had smog.
New offices and laws were made sometime in this period to watch pollution
analysis_reasoning: Overall the movement was caused by growing pollution, greater public knowledge, and distrust of businesses and government, these factors worked together as America became more modern.

prompt: Develop an argument comparing the economic policies of the Reagan and George W. Bush administrations from 1981 to 2008.
student_response:
Reagan and George Bush both had economic policies when they were president. Some of the policies were alike and some were different because they served at different times. The economy goes up and down, so a president has to decide whether to raise things or lower things. Reagan mostly wanted the economy to become stronger, and Bush also wanted that, though the results were not always the same.

Taxes were important to both of them. Reagan thought taxes could be too much and that people should keep more money. Bush also talked about taxes because Republicans usually favor reducing them. This helped business owners and regular people depending on how much they earned. Critics said rich people recieved more advantages. Supporters said when rich people invest, jobs will appear. There were debates about whether this worked, but the economy did improve at certain times.

Government spending was another subject. Reagan wanted less government but also spent money on important national programs. Bush likewise spent money while saying that private business was better. This seems contradictory but presidents cannot control every part of a budget. Congress and unexpected events matter. Both presidents had deficits, which means the government spent more than it had, although one deficit may have been larger than another.

The two administrations therefore can be compared in many ways. Reagan was earlier and his ideas influenced Republicans who came later, including Bush. Bush used similar ideas but had to adjust them to a newer economy. There were tax cuts, spending, jobs, and problems under both. Overall each president tried to help growth in a conservative way, yet no policy is exactly the same because the situations were different.

feedback.thesis: States that policies were alike and different but does not identify a specific difference or establish a developed comparative line of reasoning.
feedback.contextualization: Mentions different times and economic fluctuations only generically, without describing a broader historical setting for either administration.
feedback.evidence: Provides relevant examples of tax reduction and deficits under both presidents, but the examples remain broad and thinly developed.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: Uses comparison by discussing taxes, spending, and deficits under both administrations, though the comparison lacks depth and qualification.

thesis: Some of the policies were alike and some were different because they served at different times.
contextualization: The economy goes up and down, so a president has to decide whether to raise things or lower things.
evidence: Reagan thought taxes could be too much and that people should keep more money. Bush also talked about taxes because Republicans usually favor reducing them.
analysis_reasoning: Both presidents had deficits, which means the government spent more than it had, although one deficit may have been larger than another.

feedback.thesis: The response says the policies were alike and different but never identifies a substantive difference, so it does not establish a clear comparative line of reasoning.
feedback.contextualization: It notes only that the presidents served at different times and that economies fluctuate, without describing broader historical context for either administration.
feedback.evidence: The essay identifies tax reduction, spending despite small-government language, and deficits under both administrations and uses them to support similarity.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response uses basic comparison by organizing around taxes, spending, and deficits, but it does not develop complexity.

thesis: Some of the policies were alike and some were different because they served at different times.
contextualization: The economy goes up and down, so a president has to decide whether to raise things or lower things.
evidence: There were tax cuts, spending, jobs, and problems under both.
Both presidents had deficits, which means the government spent more than it had
analysis_reasoning: Reagan wanted less government but also spent money on important national programs. Bush likewise spent money while saying that private business was better.

prompt: Develop an argument evaluating continuity and change in national political authority from 1776 to 1800.
student_response:
From 1776 to 1800, national political authority changed from a deliberately weak union controlled largely by the states into a stronger federal system able to tax, regulate, and enforce national decisions, while suspicion of centralized power continued to limit what the new government could claim. The result was greater national capacity without a complete rejection of revolutionary republican fears.

Americans had recently experienced a much more important struggle over national authority during the Civil War. Abraham Lincoln insisted that states could not leave the Union, and after victory the national government supervised Reconstruction in the South. A set of amendments ended slavery and established national citizenship. These developments prove that war can strengthen a government when states resist it. But this happened after the early republic. A country facing crisis usually moves toward centralized authority. Fragments of power. Arguments about liberty.

Economic expansion also forced national decisions. Thomas Jefferson purchased Louisiana and doubled the nation's land even though he had often spoken for a narrow reading of federal power. Later, Andrew Jackson destroyed a national bank and claimed to represent the common people. The market grew through canals and railroads, and the federal government had to decide whether it would support internal improvements. These examples show an ongoing disagreement between national planning and local control. Mostly from later decades. Still the same pattern.

A stronger national government can also appear during economic emergency. Franklin Roosevelt's New Deal created agencies that regulated banks, employed workers, and supported farmers. The Supreme Court considered whether these actions exceeded federal authority. This was a continuation of the old question about what a republic permits the center to do. Much later than 1800. Yet helpful for seeing the issue.

Foreign danger mattered too. When Britain attacked American shipping, President Madison asked for war, and the War of 1812 encouraged nationalism. The Monroe Doctrine then warned European countries to stay out of the Western Hemisphere. A national executive had to speak for the whole country rather than fifty separate states. These events occurred once national institutions had already grown. They reveal the direction of change.

At the same time, Americans repeatedly resisted authority. Southern nullifiers, abolitionists opposing the Fugitive Slave Act, Populists attacking eastern finance, and civil rights protestors all accused the national system of serving the wrong interests. The exact policies changed, but fear of distant power continued. Short, forceful objections. State rights. Individual rights.

Therefore the era from independence through 1800 should be understood as both change and continuity. National leaders acquired more practical authority than revolutionary Americans first expected, especially over revenue and enforcement, but they could not erase state power or popular distrust. This same tension returned during nearly every later period of American history. The national government became stronger in form while remaining contested in political culture.

feedback.thesis: States a defensible continuity-and-change claim about stronger federal capacity alongside continuing distrust of centralized power.
feedback.contextualization: The surrounding discussion focuses on later events rather than a broader setting relevant to 1776-1800.
feedback.evidence: Provides no specific evidence from 1776 to 1800; its examples come from later periods that the essay itself identifies as later.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: Although the thesis invokes continuity and change, the body does not use evidence from the assigned period to structure that historical reasoning.

thesis: From 1776 to 1800, national political authority changed from a deliberately weak union controlled largely by the states into a stronger federal system able to tax, regulate, and enforce national decisions, while suspicion of centralized power continued to limit what the new government could claim.
contextualization: Americans had recently experienced a much more important struggle over national authority during the Civil War.
evidence: Mostly from later decades. Still the same pattern.
Much later than 1800. Yet helpful for seeing the issue.
analysis_reasoning: The exact policies changed, but fear of distant power continued. Short, forceful objections. State rights. Individual rights.

feedback.thesis: The opening directly argues that national authority became stronger while distrust of centralization continued to limit it.
feedback.contextualization: The response does not establish relevant broader context for 1776–1800; its contextual discussion centers on much later developments.
feedback.evidence: The essay supplies no specific evidence from 1776–1800, instead explicitly labeling several examples as later than the period.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response consistently frames its claim through continuity and change, but the off-period examples prevent a complex, well-supported analysis.

thesis: From 1776 to 1800, national political authority changed from a deliberately weak union controlled largely by the states into a stronger federal system able to tax, regulate, and enforce national decisions, while suspicion of centralized power continued to limit what the new government could claim.
contextualization: Americans had recently experienced a much more important struggle over national authority during the Civil War.
evidence: But this happened after the early republic.
Mostly from later decades. Still the same pattern.
analysis_reasoning: The national government became stronger in form while remaining contested in political culture.
